question.text: What is the threshold for determining whether a derivative instrument issued is an Offshore Derivative Instrument (ODI) for trades with non-proprietary indices as underlying?

answer.text: 20% - trades where Indian underlying is less than 20% of the index would not be regarded as ODIs
10% - trades where Indian underlying is less than 10% of the index would not be regarded as ODIs
25% - trades where Indian underlying is less than 25% of the index would not be regarded as ODIs
15% - trades where Indian underlying is less than 15% of the index would not be regarded as ODIs

explanation.text: **The Logic:** As per SEBI master circular, in determining whether a derivative instrument is an ODI, the threshold for trades with non-proprietary indices (e.g., MSCI World or MSCI EM Asia) as underlying shall be 20%. Trades where Indian underlying is less than 20% of the index would not be regarded as ODIs, even if hedged onshore. **The Trap:** Custom baskets as underlying would always be regarded as ODIs regardless of the percentage of Indian component hedged onshore. The 20% threshold only applies to non-proprietary indices. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This distinction between non-proprietary indices and custom baskets is critical for compliance officers to assess ODI classification correctly.
